planting.note: Divide established clumps in early fall or early spring, ensuring each division has several eyes and a portion of the rhizome; wild geranium can also be grown from seed sown in fall to cold-stratify naturally over winter, though seedlings take 2 years to reach flowering size — fall division is the most reliable propagation method for garden use.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; wild geranium is one of the finest native spring wildflowers of the eastern deciduous forest, producing deeply lobed, palmately divided leaves and clusters of five-petaled, rose-pink to magenta flowers from April through June — it blooms during the critical window before the tree canopy fully leafs out, making it an ideal companion for spring ephemerals and shade-garden perennials. Native across a broad range from Manitoba to Georgia and west to Kansas, it is among the most adaptable of eastern woodland wildflowers, tolerating a wide range of soil pH, moderate summer drought once established, and everything from dappled shade under deciduous trees to fairly deep shade — the foliage often takes on attractive reddish-autumn tones before dying back. The deeply lobed leaves provide attractive foliage from spring through frost and the plant slowly spreads by rhizome to form modest, well-behaved colonies; it is an excellent substitute for non-native Geranium species in shaded native gardens and is an important early-season pollen source for native bees, particularly specialist mining bees. Plant in groups of three or more for the best floral display and allow it to naturalize in a woodland garden setting where it will gradually form a loose, low-maintenance groundcover under deciduous shrubs and trees.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring just as growth emerges, or take stem cuttings in early summer; Carolina phlox is best propagated vegetatively by division or cuttings rather than seed, as seedlings may not come true to type and take 2 years to reach bloom — divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent center die-out.

directions: Plant in average to moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Carolina phlox is a superior native alternative to the widely grown garden phlox (Phlox paniculata), closely resembling it in flower form and color but distinguished by its thicker, glossier, lanceolate leaves and significantly better resistance to powdery mildew — the common epithet 'thick-leaf phlox' reflects the noticeably more substantial leaf texture that imparts this disease resistance. Native to the southeastern United States from North Carolina to Georgia and westward, it produces dense, domed panicles of fragrant, five-petaled flowers in bright pink to purple-pink from June through August, providing critical mid-summer nectar for swallowtail butterflies, sphinx moths, and ruby-throated hummingbirds. It performs best in partial shade with adequate moisture and good air circulation, which further reduces mildew pressure; plant in groups of three or more for visual impact and divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor, as older clumps tend to become woody in the center. Carolina phlox is an excellent candidate for the mid-border of native perennial gardens and woodland edge plantings, where its upright habit and long summer bloom period fill the gap between spring and late-summer wildflowers.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as growth emerges, or take stem cuttings in late spring; white turtlehead can also be grown from seed sown in fall for natural cold stratification, but germination is slow and irregular — spring division of established clumps is the most reliable propagation method; plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil and do not allow to dry out.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; white turtlehead is a native wetland and streambank perennial of eastern and central North America whose distinctive, pouched, snapdragon-like white flowers — occasionally tinged with pink at the tips — are held in dense, terminal spikes from August through October, providing crucial late-season nectar when few other wildflowers remain in bloom. The common name refers precisely to the flower's shape: the two lips of the corolla form a closed mouth with a curved upper lip strongly reminiscent of a turtle's head, and the flowers do not readily open further without the weight of a visiting bumblebee, which forces the corolla apart to access the nectar within — this pollination mechanism makes it nearly exclusively bumblebee-pollinated. Chelone glabra is the primary larval host plant for the Baltimore checkerspot butterfly (Euphydryas phaeton), one of the most striking and ecologically sensitive native butterflies of the eastern United States, making it an essential plant for conservation-minded gardeners in its native range. It spreads moderately by rhizomes and self-seeding in reliably moist to wet conditions and is an outstanding choice for rain gardens, pond and stream margins, wet woodland edges, and any consistently wet garden situation in partial shade.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as growth resumes, or sow seed in fall after scarification for natural cold stratification; Hubricht's bluestar is slow from seed but long-lived and resents frequent disturbance once established — choose a permanent location in average, well-drained soil in full sun and allow clumps to develop undisturbed for maximum ornamental effect and the spectacular golden-yellow fall foliage color.

directions: Plant in average to lean, well-drained soil in full sun; Hubricht's bluestar is widely acclaimed as one of the finest native perennials in cultivation and is arguably the single best native perennial for fall foliage color — from late September through October the densely mounded, needle-like, thread-fine foliage turns a spectacular luminous golden-yellow that rivals the best of the woody shrubs and trees, providing weeks of brilliant autumn color even in warm climates where other fall color plants underperform. In spring, dense clusters of small, pale blue, star-shaped flowers cover the mounded plant before the full foliage effect develops, providing early-season nectar for native bees; the feathery, almost grass-like texture of the foliage (individual leaves can be 3 inches long and barely a millimeter wide) creates an extraordinary fine-textured mass throughout the growing season. Native to a very restricted natural range in the Ouachita Mountains of Arkansas and eastern Oklahoma, it was discovered by amateur botanist Leslie Hubricht in 1942 and has since become one of the most celebrated native perennials in American horticulture, earning the Perennial Plant Association's Plant of the Year award in 2011. It is exceptionally tough, drought-tolerant once established, deer-resistant, and essentially pest- and disease-free — a low-maintenance perennial of outstanding three-season ornamental value that deserves far wider use in the native landscape.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring, or sow seed in fall after scarifying the seed coat for natural cold stratification over winter; thread-leaf bluestar resents frequent disturbance once established and is best sited permanently in lean, very well-drained, sandy or rocky soil in full sun — avoid heavy clay or consistently moist soils, which cause root rot and poor performance.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, sandy or rocky, dry to average soil in full sun; thread-leaf bluestar is closely related to Hubricht's bluestar (A. hubrichtii) and shares its extraordinary fine-textured, thread-like foliage and brilliant golden-yellow fall color, but has a broader natural range across the sandy pinelands, rocky outcrops, and dry prairies of the southeastern United States from North Carolina to Texas, making it better adapted to heat and more drought-tolerant than its Ozark relative. In spring, before the foliage matures, it produces terminal clusters of small, pale powder-blue, star-shaped flowers that are excellent nectar sources for native bees and early butterflies; throughout the growing season the mounded, almost needle-like foliage provides an exceptional fine-textured contrast to broader-leaved perennials in the sunny border or native meadow planting. Thread-leaf bluestar is distinguished from A. hubrichtii by the ciliate (fringed with fine hairs) margins of its leaves — hence the epithet ciliata — and by its somewhat broader ecological amplitude in dry, sandy, infertile soils where it is extremely long-lived and essentially self-sufficient once established. It is deer-resistant, pest- and disease-free, and tolerates both the heat and humidity of the South and the cold of Zone 5, making it one of the most adaptable and underused native perennials for challenging dry, sunny conditions across the eastern and central United States.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Surface-sow seed in fall directly onto moist, calcareous, sandy or mucky soil at the water's edge without covering (seed requires light for germination); brook lobelia seed is tiny and needs moisture and cold stratification to germinate reliably — fall sowing in a permanently moist spot along a stream bank, limestone seep, or pond margin allows natural stratification and spring germination; do not allow the seedbed to dry out at any point.

directions: Plant in consistently wet, calcareous to neutral, sandy or peaty soil in partial sun to light shade; brook lobelia is a small, delicate native perennial of limestone fens, calcareous seeps, wet meadows, and stream banks across boreal and northern North America from Newfoundland to British Columbia south to New Jersey and the Great Lakes region — it inhabits some of the most specialized and ecologically sensitive wetland habitats on the continent, particularly calcium-rich fens and spring seeps where few other wildflowers can compete. Despite its diminutive stature of 6–12 inches, it is an exquisite plant when in bloom from June through August, producing a loose raceme of sky-blue, two-lipped flowers on wiry, leafy stems that emerge from a basal rosette of small, spoon-shaped leaves; the flowers are structurally identical to the more familiar great blue lobelia (L. siphilitica) but in miniature, with the same characteristic slit in the upper petals and protruding anther column. Lobelia kalmii is named in honor of Pehr Kalm, the Finnish-Swedish botanist and student of Linnaeus who traveled extensively in North America from 1748 to 1751 and collected many of the specimens that became the basis for Linnaeus's descriptions of North American plants; it is one of the most cold-hardy lobelias, surviving the deep winters of USDA Zone 2. It is best suited to naturalistic wet garden settings such as bog gardens, wet meadow margins, and the edges of naturalistic ponds and streams where it can colonize moist, calcareous soils with minimal competition, and is smaller and more refined in appearance than either L. siphilitica or L. cardinalis.
